planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings (not seed) taken from established plants. Root cuttings in moist potting mix and transplant in fall for establishment over winter. In zone 9 tree collards grow year-round as a woody perennial shrub, reaching 6–8 feet tall, and produce harvestable leaves every month if watered consistently.

directions: Root stem cuttings 6–8 inches long in moist potting mix or directly in garden soil; tree collards are essentially perennial collards that develop a woody trunk over time and can produce harvestable leaves year-round in mild-winter climates. The purple-leaf variety is the most common and most ornamental, with deep blue-green to purple foliage that intensifies in color after cool weather. Harvest the large lower leaves from the bottom of the plant up, leaving the growing tip intact; consistent harvest keeps plants productive and prevents them from becoming leggy. Unlike annual collards, tree collards do not bolt and can remain productive for 5–10 years with occasional hard pruning to rejuvenate the growth.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Perennial Vegetables for California
UC Santa Cruz Center for Agroecology and Sustainable Food Systems

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in zone 9 for a winter and spring crop; mitsuba prefers cool, moist conditions and partial shade. It bolts in summer heat but self-seeds readily and returns from the base or seed each fall. Seeds require cold stratification or fall sowing to break dormancy.

directions: Sow in partial to full shade in moist, fertile, humus-rich soil — mitsuba is one of the few culinary herbs that genuinely thrives in shade and suffers in direct summer sun. It prefers conditions similar to cilantro or chervil: cool, consistently moist, and sheltered from afternoon heat. Harvest the trifoliate leaves and stems when young and tender; the flavor is a delicate blend of celery, parsley, and mild anise and is used extensively in Japanese cuisine as a garnish for clear soups (suimono), chawanmushi (egg custard), and in hot pot dishes. In zone 9 it is best treated as a cool-season annual, sowing in October and harvesting through April before heat causes bolting.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asian Herbs
Cornell University Small Farms Program — Asian Specialty Vegetables

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after soil warms to 65°F; heliconia establishes best in the warmest months. In zone 9 the tops are killed by hard freezes but established rhizomes resprout reliably in spring. Mulch heavily in winter to insulate the rhizomes.

directions: Plant in rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil in full sun to bright partial shade; parrot heliconia is one of the smaller and hardiest heliconias, tolerating more shade and cold than the larger species. The upright orange, red, and yellow bracts — technically modified leaves rather than flowers — are spectacular cut flowers lasting 2–3 weeks in arrangements. The true flowers are held inside the bracts and serve as nectar sources for hummingbirds. Water consistently and fertilize monthly during the growing season with a balanced fertilizer to maintain lush growth and continuous flowering; reduce watering and stop feeding as temperatures cool in fall. Clumps spread by underground rhizomes and can be divided every 2–3 years.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia Species
University of Puerto Rico Agricultural Experiment Station — Tropical Ornamentals

planting.note: Plant sets (small bulbs) in fall — September through November in zone 9 — for harvest the following spring. Multiplier onions form clusters of 4–12 small bulbs underground; the cluster is divided at harvest, with the largest bulbs eaten and the smallest saved for replanting. They can also be planted in late winter for a summer harvest.

directions: Plant small sets 1–2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; multiplier onions are a perennial system that perpetuates itself through vegetative division rather than seed. One planted bulb will produce a cluster of 4–12 bulbs at harvest; sort the cluster at harvest, eating the largest and replanting the smallest to maintain the planting year after year. The flavor is milder and sweeter than common onions, and the green tops can be harvested as scallions throughout the growing season. In zone 9 they perform best planted in fall; in hot, dry summers the tops die down and the bulbs go dormant, much like garlic. This heirloom vegetable was once widely grown in kitchen gardens before commercial onions became cheap.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Multiplier Onion Growing Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Onions in the Home Garden
Seed Savers Exchange — Allium cepa var. aggregatum

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; luffa needs a long season (150+ days frost-free) and benefits from the head start in all but the Deep South. In zone 9 the long summer provides ample time for full maturation when started in March. Direct sow is possible in late March–April.

directions: Grow on a strong trellis, arbor, or fence — luffa vines are extremely vigorous and can cover a large structure in a single season. The large yellow flowers are showy and attract bees and pollinators. Young fruits at 4–6 inches long are eaten as a tender vegetable in Asian cuisines (similar to zucchini but with a silkier texture); for sponge production, allow fruits to mature fully on the vine until the skin turns brown and papery, then soak in water to loosen and remove the outer skin, revealing the fibrous interior skeleton. Dry the sponge in the sun and rinse several times until the fiber is clean and pale yellow. Each vine can produce dozens of sponges.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Luffa
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Luffa Gourd
